<findings>

[observation] [info] High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
[observation] [info] Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling confirm this zone is fully self-contained — all e2e test files import only from each other (via e2e-helpers.js) and from node builtins, with no cross-zone dependencies.
[observation] [info] The three architectural guardrail tests (architecture-policy, domain-isolation, boundary-check in web package) form an intentionally distributed policy suite — comments in each file cross-reference the others. This pattern keeps enforcement logic close to the tier being enforced while maintaining discoverability.
[observation] [info] verify-build.js is a non-test utility script colocated in the e2e directory. If it is run outside of vitest (e.g. directly via node), it belongs alongside CI tooling at the root rather than in the test directory, to avoid confusion about whether it is part of the test suite.
[pattern] [info] The architectural guardrail suite is split across three files in two packages (architecture-policy.test.js + domain-isolation.test.js in e2e-policy-suite; boundary-check.test.ts in the web package). The split is documented via @see cross-references, but there is no single authoritative entry point that names all three files. A new contributor auditing architectural enforcement must follow the comment chain to discover the full suite — consider adding a guardrails index or a top-level comment in CLAUDE.md listing all three files explicitly.
[relationship] [info] The e2e-policy-suite enforces structural invariants for all other zones but has zero coupling to them — it reads the filesystem at test-time rather than importing production modules. This means the policy tests cannot be broken by production refactors, but they also cannot catch violations introduced inside dist/ artifacts that differ from src/. Any architectural guardrail that requires runtime import graph traversal (rather than source-level grep) falls outside this zone's coverage.
[anti-pattern] [info] Policy/guardrail tests and CLI behavioral tests share the same zone and are reported together in CI output. A failing CLI behavioral test (e.g. changed flag name) produces the same test-failure signal as a failing architectural invariant, making severity triage harder. Separating guardrail tests into a distinct entry (e.g. a separate vitest project or explicit tag) would allow CI to treat policy failures as blocking while CLI behavioral failures are treated as warnings.
[anti-pattern] [warning] verify-build.js is a standalone node script colocated with vitest test files but not executed by vitest — it is excluded from test coverage and CI gating unless explicitly invoked separately. Scripts not managed by the test runner should live alongside CI tooling (ci.js, root scripts/) to make their execution contract explicit.
[suggestion] [info] Add verify-build.js to the vitest.config exclude list to make its non-test status explicit and auditable, preventing accidental inclusion in future test runs if the file gains test-like exports.
[suggestion] [info] Adopt an explicit file naming convention for the zone: use 'policy-' prefix for structural invariant tests (policy-architecture, policy-domain-isolation) and keep 'cli-' prefix for behavioral tests. Document the convention in a comment at the top of e2e-helpers.js so new contributors know which prefix to apply.
[suggestion] [info] Migrate e2e-helpers.js to e2e-helpers.ts to bring it under TypeScript type checking. The file defines the shared harness API consumed by all 14+ test files — type errors in its parameters or return values currently propagate silently until caught by call-site inference.

</findings>

<insights>

- High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
- The zone contains two distinct test categories: policy/guardrail tests (architecture-policy, domain-isolation, integration-coverage-policy) that verify structural invariants, and CLI behavioral tests (cli-*.test.js) that verify command-line contracts — keeping these together is appropriate since they share the e2e-helpers.js harness
- The shared e2e-helpers.js module centralizes temp directory lifecycle, CLI path resolution, and process spawn wrappers across 14+ test files, preventing duplication and making the harness easy to extend
- verify-build.js is a non-test build verification script colocated with tests — it should be reviewed to ensure it belongs here and is not more naturally a CI script at the root level
- Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling confirm this zone is fully self-contained — all e2e test files import only from each other (via e2e-helpers.js) and from node builtins, with no cross-zone dependencies.
- The three architectural guardrail tests (architecture-policy, domain-isolation, boundary-check in web package) form an intentionally distributed policy suite — comments in each file cross-reference the others. This pattern keeps enforcement logic close to the tier being enforced while maintaining discoverability.
- verify-build.js is a non-test utility script colocated in the e2e directory. If it is run outside of vitest (e.g. directly via node), it belongs alongside CI tooling at the root rather than in the test directory, to avoid confusion about whether it is part of the test suite.
- The zone's zero coupling to all production zones is architecturally load-bearing: test infrastructure changes are completely firewalled from production code, eliminating any risk of test-helper code polluting production build graphs.
- The cross-reference comments in architecture-policy.test.js (@see domain-isolation.test.js, @see boundary-check.test.ts) reveal that the guardrail suite is intentionally split across three test files in two packages — this distributed-but-cross-referenced pattern is a deliberate tradeoff: enforcement stays close to the tier it guards, but discoverability requires following the @see chain.
- The 22-file test zone enforces structural invariants across a 5-package monorepo with no runtime dependency on any production zone (coupling: 0) — this is the only zone in the graph where high file count correlates with zero coupling, confirming the isolation is intentional and not a measurement artifact.
- The e2e-policy-suite enforces structural invariants for all other zones but has zero coupling to them — it reads the filesystem at test-time rather than importing production modules. This means the policy tests cannot be broken by production refactors, but they also cannot catch violations introduced inside dist/ artifacts that differ from src/. Any architectural guardrail that requires runtime import graph traversal (rather than source-level grep) falls outside this zone's coverage.
- The architectural guardrail suite is split across three files in two packages (architecture-policy.test.js + domain-isolation.test.js in e2e-policy-suite; boundary-check.test.ts in the web package). The split is documented via @see cross-references, but there is no single authoritative entry point that names all three files. A new contributor auditing architectural enforcement must follow the comment chain to discover the full suite — consider adding a guardrails index or a top-level comment in CLAUDE.md listing all three files explicitly.
- The zone mixes two test categories with different failure modes: policy/guardrail tests (architecture-policy, domain-isolation) fail only when architectural invariants change, while CLI behavioral tests (cli-*.test.js) fail on any CLI output or flag change. Mixing them inflates flakiness signal — a noisy CLI behavioral failure obscures whether a policy test passed or failed in the same run.
- verify-build.js has no vitest exports and is not discovered by the test runner; it is silently excluded from coverage and CI pass/fail gating unless explicitly invoked. Its presence in the e2e directory creates a false impression that it is part of the guarded test suite.
- verify-build.js is a standalone node script colocated with vitest test files but not executed by vitest — it is excluded from test coverage and CI gating unless explicitly invoked separately. Scripts not managed by the test runner should live alongside CI tooling (ci.js, root scripts/) to make their execution contract explicit.
- Policy/guardrail tests and CLI behavioral tests share the same zone and are reported together in CI output. A failing CLI behavioral test (e.g. changed flag name) produces the same test-failure signal as a failing architectural invariant, making severity triage harder. Separating guardrail tests into a distinct entry (e.g. a separate vitest project or explicit tag) would allow CI to treat policy failures as blocking while CLI behavioral failures are treated as warnings.
- The zone contains two distinct file naming conventions with no documented rule: policy/guardrail tests use descriptive hyphenated names (architecture-policy, domain-isolation, integration-coverage-policy) while CLI behavioral tests use a 'cli-' prefix (cli-arg-contracts, cli-ci, cli-config). New contributors adding tests have no signal for which convention to follow, risking drift toward a third ad-hoc pattern.
- e2e-helpers.js uses the .js extension while the rest of the monorepo uses .ts — the shared harness file is the only non-TypeScript source file in a TypeScript-first monorepo. It receives no type checking, and any type errors in its API surface are only caught at call sites in .ts files, not at the definition.
- verify-build.js is not listed in any vitest exclude pattern — its exclusion from test discovery is implicit (vitest skips files without test exports) rather than explicit. Adding it to vitest.config's exclude array would make the exclusion auditable and prevent a future contributor from accidentally adding a describe() block to it.
- Adopt an explicit file naming convention for the zone: use 'policy-' prefix for structural invariant tests (policy-architecture, policy-domain-isolation) and keep 'cli-' prefix for behavioral tests. Document the convention in a comment at the top of e2e-helpers.js so new contributors know which prefix to apply.
- Migrate e2e-helpers.js to e2e-helpers.ts to bring it under TypeScript type checking. The file defines the shared harness API consumed by all 14+ test files — type errors in its parameters or return values currently propagate silently until caught by call-site inference.
- Add verify-build.js to the vitest.config exclude list to make its non-test status explicit and auditable, preventing accidental inclusion in future test runs if the file gains test-like exports.
- [call graph] 453 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

</insights>
